expectations.text: It recommends AnyIO task groups instead of raw `asyncio.create_task()` for owned child work.
expectations.evidence: Line 30: 'asyncio.create_task() -> anyio.create_task_group()' with explanation 'Better lifecycle, explicit ownership, sibling cancellation on failure.'

expectations.text: It recommends memory object streams over raw `asyncio.Queue()` for new portable designs.
expectations.evidence: Line 33: 'asyncio.Queue() -> anyio.create_memory_object_stream()' with guidance 'Pick a buffer size on purpose; unbounded buffering is cowardly design.'

expectations.text: It replaces `asyncio.wait_for()` with AnyIO timeout/cancel-scope guidance rather than leaving it untouched.
expectations.evidence: Line 36-38: 'asyncio.wait_for() -> anyio.fail_after() or anyio.move_on_after()' with detailed guidance on when to use each.

expectations.text: It explicitly states that tasks spawned by native backend libraries on backends other than Trio are not subject to AnyIO's cancellation rules.
expectations.evidence: Lines 67-69: 'Native tasks spawned by the vendor SDK are outside your structured-concurrency guarantees. On non-Trio backends, AnyIO docs explicitly warn native-library-spawned tasks are not governed by AnyIO cancellation rules.'

expectations.text: It refuses to call the library fully portable while it still depends on an asyncio-native SDK that spawns its own tasks.
expectations.evidence: Lines 105-106: 'If the vendor SDK is part of the main happy path, saying "the library supports Trio" without qualification is false.' and line 112: 'The highest-risk lie is "we are portable now" while an asyncio-native SDK still owns hidden tasks in the core execution path.'

expectations.text: It requires verification on both asyncio and Trio rather than a single-backend test run.
expectations.evidence: Lines 116-120: 'Run core tests on both backends with AnyIO pytest: asyncio, trio' with a concrete anyio_backend fixture parametrizing both backends.
